name: Nuclei
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. Composition and Size of the Nucleus
sections.body:
The nucleus contains protons (charge +$e$, mass $m_p \approx 1.673 \times 10^{-27}$ kg) and neutrons (neutral, $m_n \approx 1.675 \times 10^{-27}$ kg). Collectively called nucleons.

Notation: $^A_Z X$, where $Z$ = atomic number (protons), $A$ = mass number (nucleons), $N = A - Z$ = neutrons. Examples: $^1_1\text{H}$, $^4_2\text{He}$, $^{235}_{92}\text{U}$.

Classifications:

  • Isotopes: same $Z$, different $A$ (e.g. $^1$H, $^2$D, $^3$T).
  • Isobars: same $A$, different $Z$ (e.g. $^3_1$H, $^3_2$He).
  • Isotones: same $N$, different $Z$ (e.g. $^3_1$H, $^4_2$He).

Nuclear radius: $$R = R_0 A^{1/3}$$ with $R_0 \approx 1.2$ fm. Nuclear density is constant ($\rho \approx 2.3 \times 10^{17}$ kg/m┬│) ŌĆö independent of $A$ ŌĆö implying nucleons are tightly packed and short-range nuclear force saturates.

sections.heading: 2. Atomic Mass Unit and Mass-Energy Equivalence
sections.body:
Atomic mass unit: $1\,\text{u} = (1/12)$ ├Ś mass of $^{12}$C atom $= 1.6605 \times 10^{-27}$ kg.

Einstein's mass-energy relation: $$E = mc^2$$ Mass-energy equivalent of 1 u: $E = 1\text{u}\cdot c^2 = 931.5$ MeV.

So small mass changes ($\Delta m \sim$ u) correspond to large energies ($\Delta E \sim$ MeV) ŌĆö characteristic of nuclear processes, vs. eV in chemistry.

sections.heading: 3. Mass Defect and Binding Energy
sections.body:
The mass of any nucleus is less than the sum of free nucleon masses. Mass defect: $$\Delta m = [Z m_p + (A-Z) m_n] - M_{\text{nucleus}}$$

Binding energy: energy needed to separate nucleus into free nucleons (or equivalently, energy released when free nucleons assemble): $$E_b = \Delta m \cdot c^2$$

Binding energy per nucleon $E_b/A$: a measure of stability. Plot vs. $A$:

  • Rises rapidly for light nuclei.
  • Peaks around $A \approx 56$ (Fe) at ~8.8 MeV/nucleon ŌĆö most stable.
  • Falls slowly for heavier nuclei.
  • Average for medium/heavy nuclei: ~8 MeV/nucleon.

This shape implies energy is released either by fusion of light nuclei (climbing toward Fe) or by fission of heavy nuclei (also climbing toward Fe).

sections.heading: 4. Nuclear Force
sections.body:
The strong nuclear force binds nucleons together against electrostatic repulsion among protons. Properties:

  • Short-range: significant only within ~3 fm; negligible beyond.
  • Much stronger than electromagnetic at short range (~100├Ś).
  • Charge-independent: same between p-p, n-n, p-n.
  • Attractive at typical separations (~1 fm), strongly repulsive at very short distances (<0.5 fm) ŌĆö preventing nuclear collapse.
  • Saturates: each nucleon interacts only with its nearest neighbours, hence constant binding energy per nucleon.
  • Spin-dependent.

sections.heading: 5. Radioactivity
sections.body:
Spontaneous emission of radiation from unstable nuclei. Discovered by Becquerel (1896). Three natural types:

  • Alpha (╬▒): $^4_2$He nucleus. Heavy, low penetration (paper stops); ionises strongly. $^A_Z X \to ^{A-4}_{Z-2} Y + ^4_2\text{He}$.
  • Beta (╬▓Ōü╗): high-energy electron from neutron decay $n \to p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e$ in nucleus. Continuous energy spectrum (due to neutrino sharing energy). $^A_Z X \to ^A_{Z+1} Y + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e$. ╬▓Ōü║ (positron) also exists.
  • Gamma (╬│): high-energy photon from de-excitation of nucleus. $A$, $Z$ unchanged; very penetrating (lead needed).

Soddy-Fajans displacement laws state how $A$, $Z$ change for each decay type.

sections.heading: 6. Law of Radioactive Decay
sections.body:
The number of decays per unit time is proportional to the number of unstable nuclei present: $$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$ Solution: $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$, where $\lambda$ is the decay constant.

Half-life: time for half the nuclei to decay: $$T_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

Mean (average) life: $\tau = 1/\lambda = T_{1/2}/\ln 2 = 1.44\,T_{1/2}$.

Activity: $A = -dN/dt = \lambda N$. Unit: becquerel (Bq) = 1 disintegration/s. Older unit: curie (Ci) = $3.7\times 10^{10}$ Bq.

After $n$ half-lives, $N = N_0/2^n$.

sections.heading: 7. Nuclear Fission
sections.body:
A heavy nucleus (typically $^{235}$U) absorbs a slow neutron and splits into two roughly equal lighter nuclei plus 2-3 neutrons, releasing ~200 MeV per fission: $$^{235}_{92}\text{U} + n \to ^{141}_{56}\text{Ba} + ^{92}_{36}\text{Kr} + 3n + \sim 200\,\text{MeV}$$

Energy comes from increase in $E_b/A$ from $^{235}$U (~7.6 MeV) to fragments (~8.5 MeV) ŌåÆ ~0.9 MeV/nucleon ├Ś 235 Ōēł 200 MeV per fission.

The released neutrons can trigger further fissions ŌĆö a chain reaction. Controlled chain (k = 1) in nuclear reactors; uncontrolled (k > 1) in atom bombs. Reactors use control rods (cadmium/boron, absorbing neutrons) and moderators (heavy water, graphite, slowing neutrons to thermal energies).

sections.heading: 8. Nuclear Fusion
sections.body:
Two light nuclei combine to form a heavier nucleus, releasing energy. Example (proton-proton chain in stars): $$4\,^1_1\text{H} \to ^4_2\text{He} + 2e^+ + 2\nu_e + 26.7\,\text{MeV}$$

Requires extreme temperature ($\sim 10^7$ K) for nuclei to overcome Coulomb repulsion ŌĆö hence thermonuclear reactions. Source of energy in the Sun and stars; also in hydrogen bombs.

Per nucleon, fusion releases more energy than fission. Achieving controlled fusion on Earth (tokamaks, ICF) is an ongoing engineering challenge ŌĆö confining plasma at million-degree temperatures.

summary: Nucleus has $A$ nucleons in radius $R = R_0 A^{1/3}$. Mass defect $\Delta m$ gives binding energy $E_b = \Delta m c^2$, peaking at $\sim 8.8$ MeV/nucleon for Fe. Strong, short-range nuclear force binds nucleons. Decay law $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$, half-life $T_{1/2} = 0.693/\lambda$. Three decay modes: ╬▒, ╬▓, ╬│. Both fission of heavy nuclei and fusion of light nuclei release energy by climbing the $E_b/A$ curve.
pitfalls: Mass defect is a TINY mass difference (Ōēł MeV / 931 u), but yields HUGE energies because of c┬▓.
Binding energy means how much energy is REQUIRED to break apart, not what's stored in the nucleus.
╬▓Ōü╗ comes with antineutrino, ╬▓Ōü║ with neutrino ŌĆö needed for energy and lepton-number conservation.
Half-life is INDEPENDENT of initial amount, temperature, pressure, chemical state.
Fusion releases more energy per nucleon than fission, but requires extreme temperatures.
Activity = ╬╗N ŌĆö depends on amount left, NOT just on time.
